title: Chapter 27: The Devil's Bargain
content: The knock came at 7:43 p.m.

Aline knew the exact time because she'd been staring at the microwave clock, watching the digits flip while she waited for ramen water to boil, and the sound cut through the kitchen like a blade. Not Mags — Mags was in the living room. Not a delivery — she hadn't ordered anything. The knock came again, measured and deliberate, the knock of someone who expected the door to open.
She moved to the entryway on instinct, her hand finding the deadbolt but not turning it. She leaned to the peephole.
Marcus Webb smiled back at her.

The distortion of the fish-eye lens made his features bulge slightly, but there was no mistaking that smile — unhurried, proprietary, like he was already inside. He held a bottle of wine in his left hand. A Sancerre, pale gold, the label turned just so. Like he was dropping in on a friend.
The audacity stole the air from her lungs.
"Aline." His voice came muffled through the door. Not a question. A greeting. "I know you're there. I can see the light under the door."

She didn't open it. She cracked it — the chain still fastened — and no more than four inches of gap. The hallway light from her apartment fell across his face, illuminating the silver at his temples, the crisp lapels of his charcoal coat. His cologne drifted through the crack — sandalwood and bergamot, something green beneath it, expensive and deliberate. The smell of boardrooms and leverage.
Behind her, she heard Mags's footsteps stop at the end of the hallway. Aline didn't turn. She kept her eyes on Marcus through the gap.
"I'm here to make you rich and free," he said. "Five minutes."
"You have a lot of nerve showing up at my door."
"I have a lot of things, Aline. Nerve is the least interesting among them." He held up the wine bottle. "This is a 2019 Didier Dagueneau. I thought we could drink to new beginnings."
"I'm not letting you in."

"I'm not asking you to." He gestured toward the stoop outside the building's front entrance, visible through the glass of the stairwell door behind him. "Five minutes on the steps. Fresh air. You can scream if I say something you don't like."

She should have said no. Every instinct that had kept her alive through the trial, through the depositions, through the media circus, told her to close the door and call her lawyer. But Marcus Webb hadn't climbed three flights of stairs and carried a hundred-dollar bottle of wine to be told no. He'd come because he knew something she didn't. And that was more dangerous than anything he could say.
"Hallway light stays on," she said. "Door stays open. Mags—"
"I'm watching," Mags called from behind her, already moving to the window that overlooked the stoop.
Aline unhooked the chain.

The cold hit her like a rebuke. She'd grabbed her jacket on the way out but hadn't zipped it, and the November wind cut through the gap, finding the thin cotton of her t-shirt underneath. The stoop concrete was brutal through her jeans, and she shifted her weight, trying to find a position where the chill didn't seep up through her bones. The streetlight above them buzzed — a low, tired hum — and somewhere a car alarm barked twice and fell silent.

Marcus sat beside her, not close enough to touch but close enough that she could feel the warmth radiating off his coat. He set the wine between them, untouched, a prop. He'd never intended to open it.

"Three-album deal with Vantage," he said, without preamble. "Full creative control — I'm not interested in manufacturing your sound. A team of your choosing, marketing budget without ceiling, international tour guaranteed. The contract is already drawn. You'd be the flagship artist for the next decade."
"What's the catch?"

"You know the catch. That's why you're sitting here in the cold instead of telling me to go to hell." He paused, letting the words settle. "Sign the deal. Make a public statement — a brief one, nothing elaborate — that Cassian Mercer's surveillance was traumatic, that you've been processing the aftermath, that you're focusing on healing and your music. That's it. A few sentences."
"And in exchange?"

"I direct my contacts at the DOJ to drop all charges." He said it as simply as if he were describing the weather. "Cassian Mercer walks free. No trial, no prison sentence, no criminal record. The surveillance footage is sealed. He gets his life back."
The wind shifted, carrying the faint smell of exhaust from the street and, beneath it, the green cutting edge of Marcus's cologne. Aline's fingers had gone numb. She pressed them against her thighs.

"Everyone wins," Marcus continued. "You get a career — a real career, the kind that redefines an industry. He gets his freedom. I get the artist who's going to define the next decade." He turned to look at her, and his eyes were warm, almost kind. That was the worst part. He wasn't faking the warmth. He genuinely believed this was a gift. "Your voice on that stand was remarkable, Aline. The way you spoke about him — the complexity, the refusal to simplify. It was honest. And honesty is the most valuable currency in the world. I'm asking you to be honest again."
"About what, exactly?"
"About the cost."

She stared at the wine bottle between them, its label catching the streetlight. She thought about the witness stand. The fluorescent courtroom. The jury watching her face as she said: *He watched me because he loved me. I know that now. And I loved him back.* The truth of it, the terrifying, unsimplifyable truth that had made Marcus's lawyers squirm and the prosecutor's case falter.

If she said it was traumatic — if she said what Marcus wanted her to say — she'd be recanting. Not legally, perhaps. The statement would be crafted to leave enough ambiguity, enough plausible deniability. *I was processing. I needed time. I've come to understand the impact.* Words that could mean anything and nothing. But the public wouldn't hear nuance. The public would hear: *She was wrong. It wasn't love. It was abuse. She just couldn't admit it.*

Her testimony would become perjury, or worse — the rationalizations of a victim too broken to name her own damage. Every word she'd fought to say under oath would be reframed as the desperate self-deception of a woman who couldn't face the truth. And Cass—

Cass would hear it. He'd hear the woman he loved confirm to the world that he was what they'd always said he was. A monster. A predator. She'd be signing his soul out of one cage and into another, and this one wouldn't have walls or bars. It would be built from her own words.
She'd be the one who put him there.
"You're asking me to lie," she said.

"I'm asking you to tell a different part of the truth." Marcus's smile didn't waver. "Trauma and love aren't mutually exclusive, Aline. You know that better than anyone. You said so yourself on the stand."
"Not in the way you want me to say it."

"The way the public needs to hear it." He shifted, and for a moment the mask slipped, and she saw the engine beneath — the cold, calculating thing that had built Vantage Records from nothing and made Marcus Webb one of the most powerful men in the music industry. "You can afford principle now. You know that. But Cassian Mercer can't. You hold his freedom in your hands. That's not a threat — that's the infrastructure of the system we live in. I'm simply offering you a lever."
"And if I refuse?"

The smile held. "Then Cassian Mercer goes to trial. The surveillance footage is played for a jury. Every frame, every moment, every camera angle. The prosecution walks them through it frame by frame, and no matter what you said on that stand, no matter how beautifully you defended him, the footage speaks louder than words. He'll be convicted — the evidence is overwhelming. He'll serve eight to fifteen years."
He let the number land.

"You'll have a career anyway," he continued, and now his voice was gentle, almost tender. "You're too big to fail now. The trial made you famous. Your voice made you indispensable. Vantage or no Vantage, you'll have labels lining up. You don't need me, Aline. But you'll have it alone."
*Not a threat. A fact.*
That was what made it monstrous.
Mags appeared at the window above them, her silhouette sharp against the warm light of the apartment. She didn't knock on the glass. She just stood there, watching, a sentry.
Marcus followed Aline's gaze upward, then looked back at her. "Your friend seems protective."
"She's smarter than both of us."

"Likely." He stood, brushing off his coat. "I'll give you time to think. But not much — the DOJ doesn't wait, and neither do I. You have my number." He left the wine on the stoop, untouched. A monument to an offer she hadn't accepted.
He was halfway down the steps when she said: "Marcus."
He paused, turned.
"How do I know you can deliver? Dropping federal charges — that's not a phone call."
"No," he agreed. "It's three phone calls and a favor I've been holding for eleven years. But it's done." He held her gaze. "I don't bluff, Aline. You know that."
He walked to his car — a black sedan, sleek and anonymous — and drove away without looking back.

The detention center's phone line crackled with static, a thin hiss beneath every silence, like the sound of distance being measured. Aline pressed the receiver to her ear and stared at the worn linoleum of the visitation room wall. She'd been granted a phone call — not a visit, a call — because the system was like that: generous enough to let you hear a voice, stingy enough to make sure you couldn't see a face.
The line clicked. Then his voice.
"Aline?"

Three syllables, and her ribcage contracted. She hadn't heard his voice in twelve days. It was rougher than she remembered, slightly flattened by the phone's compression, but it was *his* — that low, measured cadence that had lived in her ear for three years through speakers and screens and the strange intimacy of surveillance made devotion.
"Cass." She closed her eyes. "I need to tell you something."
"Tell me."

She told him. All of it. Marcus on the stoop. The wine. The contract. The statement he wanted her to make. The charges that would disappear. The silence on the line stretched long enough that she could hear the static breathing between them, the electronic ghost of all those miles of wire and fiber and institutional indifference that separated them.

When Cass spoke, his voice was steady. That was the worst part. Steady and clear, as if he'd been waiting for this moment, as if he'd already thought it through and arrived at the only answer he could live with.
"Take it."
"Cass—"

"Aline." He interrupted her, and his voice cracked on the second syllable, the only fracture in the wall he'd built. "I've been in a cage for three years. I built it myself. Every camera, every wire, every moment I watched you — that was my architecture. I chose it. I don't mind spending a few more years in a cage they built. I know how to exist in one." A pause, and when he spoke again, his voice had steadied, but something underneath it trembled. "But I won't let *you* spend your life in one for me."
"That's not—" She pressed her palm flat against the wall. The linoleum was cold and faintly greasy beneath her fingers. "Cass, if I say those things — if I tell the world it was trauma—"
"Then the world will believe what it already believes. Nothing changes for me. I've been what they say I am since the day I was arrested. A label doesn't make it more true."
"It makes it true from *my* mouth."
Silence. The static widened.
"Aline." His voice, when it returned, was so quiet she almost lost it in the noise. "Take the deal. Be free. Be extraordinary." A breath. "That's all I ever wanted."

The line stayed open for three more seconds. She could hear him breathing, or maybe that was the static, or maybe that was her own lungs failing in the quiet of that visitation room. Then the click — the system severing the connection, efficient and impersonal, because the county detention center didn't care about the things people were still trying to say.
She hung up the receiver. Her hand was shaking.

She walked back to the apartment on legs that didn't feel like hers. The stairwell light was fluorescent and unforgiving, and she could smell her own fear — something metallic and thin beneath the cold air. When she pushed through the front door, Mags was at the window, her posture rigid.
Aline moved past her. She was shaking — her hands, her jaw, the fine tremor in her chest that wouldn't stop. She opened the door to go back outside, to breathe, to think, and there was Marcus.
Not at the bottom of the steps. On the sidewalk, leaning against his car, patient as a saint. He looked up. He looked at her.
She opened her mouth, and before she could speak, Mags stepped past her onto the stoop.

Mags walked down the steps with the unhurried confidence of a woman who'd spent her life protecting people who couldn't protect themselves. She stopped two stairs above Marcus and looked down at him like he was something she needed to scrape off her shoe.
"Get the hell off our property."

It wasn't loud. It wasn't dramatic. It was the most threatening thing Aline had ever heard, because Mags meant every single word, and Marcus — for the first time — seemed to hear something that wasn't a negotiation.
Then Mags turned to Aline. Her expression softened, just slightly. "Don't answer him now," she said, quiet enough that only Aline could hear. "Sleep on it. If he can wait, you can wait."

Marcus studied Mags for a long moment. Then he looked at Aline — standing on the stoop in the cold, caught between the door and the steps, between the woman who loved her and the man who wanted to own her, between two different cages that each had her name on the lock.
He nodded once. Adjusted his coat. Walked to his car.
At the curb, he turned back. The wind carried his voice clearly, sharpened by the cold.
"Forty-eight hours. Then I withdraw the offer and let the justice system handle it."
He got in the car. The engine turned over. The taillights flared red and then disappeared around the corner, swallowed by the dark.

Aline stood on the stoop. The concrete was cold beneath her feet, the air sharp in her lungs. Above her, Mags waited. Inside, the apartment was warm and amber-lit and full of nothing that could help her. Somewhere across the city, in a detention center that smelled like industrial cleaner and recycled air, Cass was sitting in a cell, choosing her freedom over his own.
Forty-eight hours.
Two cages. One choice.
